planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in spring on a trellis, fence, or arbor in full sun to partial shade; in zone 9, Pink Jasmine blooms most heavily in January–March when the cooler temperatures trigger bud set — the fragrance in a good bloom year can be detected from across a garden. Avoid heavy pruning in fall as flower buds form in fall for winter bloom. Prune immediately after flowering. In zone 9a, occasional hard freezes may kill some top growth but established vines resprout from the crown. Established vines can be invasive in mild climates — manage by removing seed pods.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile soil; Pink Jasmine blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates morning sun with afternoon shade in zone 9's summer climate, which can actually extend the blooming season. Jasminum polyanthum is a twining, evergreen vine native to the mountains of Yunnan province in southern China and Burma (Myanmar) at 5,000–9,000 foot elevations — a naturally cool-adapted plant that translates beautifully to zone 8–9 winter garden conditions. In late winter (January–March in zone 9), the vine produces clouds of intensely fragrant, star-shaped flowers that open white from deep rose-pink buds — the pink buds contrasting with the pure white open flowers creates a beautiful two-tone effect. The fragrance is intensely sweet and reminiscent of jasmine absolute — one of the most powerfully scented of all cultivated jasmines, detectable from remarkable distances on warm winter afternoons. A well-established vine covering a fence or arbor in full late-winter bloom is one of the most memorable garden sights of the zone 9 winter garden, drawing visitors to investigate the fragrance source. Pink Jasmine is also widely grown as a houseplant and patio container plant, where the fragrance of winter-blooming potted specimens fills indoor spaces. Listed as invasive in some Pacific island and mild coastal California locales where the climate allows year-round seeding.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Jasminum polyanthum (Pink Jasmine)
Royal Horticultural Society — Jasminum polyanthum (AGM plant)
LSU AgCenter — Winter-Blooming Vines for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Jasminum species for Gulf Coast gardens

common_name: Scrambling Sky Vine (Purple Thunbergia, Blue Kenya Clockvine)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in spring after last frost on a trellis, fence, or through an established shrub in full sun to partial shade; Scrambling Sky Vine grows rapidly in warm weather and blooms prolifically through the warm months with peak bloom when temperatures moderate in fall. In zone 9a, it freezes back to roots in cold winters but resprouts reliably in spring; in zone 9b-10 it is semi-evergreen and blooms nearly year-round. Excellent for climbing into established shrubs or on wire fencing.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile soil; Scrambling Sky Vine thrives in zone 9 humid warmth and produces its best bloom in late summer and fall when daytime temperatures drop below 95°F. Thunbergia battiscombei is a scrambling, semi-woody tropical vine from East Africa (Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda) in the Acanthaceae family, closely related to the widely-grown Bengal Clockvine (T. grandiflora) but producing smaller flowers in a richer, deeper purple-violet with a contrasting yellow-white throat. The flowers are trumpet-shaped, 2–3 inches in diameter, and borne singly on slender stems along the length of the twining vine — the deep violet-purple with the pale, open throat is one of the most saturated blues available in a tropical vine and blooms continuously from late spring through fall in zone 9. Unlike the enormous, vigorous T. grandiflora which can overwhelm a small space, T. battiscombei is more manageable in scale, typically reaching 10–15 feet and controllable with annual pruning. The common name 'Scrambling Sky Vine' reflects its habit of weaving its way through other shrubs and structures rather than climbing tightly around a support. An excellent choice for informal tropical gardens, butterfly gardens, and screening, where its continuous production of purple flowers provides months of interest.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Thunbergia species for Florida
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Thunbergia battiscombei
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Flowering Vines for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Thunbergia species

common_name: Forest Bell Bush (River Bells, Broad Mackaya)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in partial to full shade in consistently moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; Forest Bell Bush prefers the cool, shaded conditions of its native forest habitat and will scorch in full afternoon sun in zone 9. It blooms most heavily in winter to spring (December–March in zone 9), providing lavender flower color when the shade garden has little else in bloom. In zone 9a, hard freezes may kill top growth but established plants typically resprout from the crown in spring.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Forest Bell Bush is one of the finest shade-tolerant flowering shrubs for zone 9 Gulf Coast and Florida landscapes, providing beautiful lavender blooms in the cool season when most shade plants are not flowering. Mackaya bella is a glossy-leaved evergreen shrub native to the subtropical coastal forest and riverine bush of KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa, growing naturally in the shade of forest margins and along shaded stream banks — conditions that translate perfectly to zone 9 shaded garden beds. The attractive, large, dark-green, glossy leaves have attractive wavy margins and create an excellent textural backdrop year-round. In winter to spring, the plant produces delicate, tubular, 5-lobed flowers 2 inches long in pale lavender with darker veining — a distinctive, elegant color combination that is very different from the saturated purples of most tropical flowering shrubs. The flowers are produced in loose, airy terminal spikes over a period of 6–10 weeks. Forest Bell Bush is a monotypic genus (only one species in the genus Mackaya) in the Acanthaceae family, closely related to the Justicia and Eranthemum plants well-suited to Gulf Coast shade gardens.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute — Mackaya bella
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Shade-tolerant flowering shrubs for Florida
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Mackaya bella
LSU AgCenter — Winter-Flowering Shade Shrubs for Louisiana

common_name: Chinese Money Plant (UFO Plant, Pancake Plant, Missionary Plant)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio container plant in zone 9; Chinese Money Plant cannot tolerate frost and must be kept above 50°F. It adapts well to indoor conditions with bright indirect light. Rotate the pot quarterly to prevent the plant from leaning toward the light source. The plant produces offsets (pups) at the base and on stems — remove these with roots and pot separately to propagate. Allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings to prevent root rot.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light — Chinese Money Plant does best near a bright window but not in direct sun, which scorches the round leaves; it also tolerates somewhat lower light than most popular foliage plants. Pilea peperomioides is a small, compact, upright houseplant native to Yunnan province in southern China, first collected in 1906 and brought to Europe by Norwegian missionary Agnar Espegren in 1946 — who shared cuttings with friends and neighbors so prolifically that the plant spread throughout Scandinavia as a houseplant decades before it became formally described and commercially available in the rest of the world, earning the nickname 'Missionary Plant.' The plant is immediately recognizable by its extraordinary round, peltate leaves (the stem attaches to the center of the underside of the leaf, not the edge) in a bright, glossy, medium-green — each leaf resembling a small green pancake or shield on a thin stem. Multiple leaves on long, flexible petioles radiate from a central upright stem, creating a distinctive, architectural form unlike any other common houseplant. In recent years, Chinese Money Plant has become one of the most sought-after houseplants globally, widely shared between plant enthusiasts as pups (offsets) because it freely produces new plantlets at the base and along stems. It is non-toxic to pets and children.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Pilea peperomioides cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pilea species for indoor culture
American Nurseryman — Pilea peperomioides cultural notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Pilea (non-toxic)

common_name: Van Houtte Sage (Van Houtte's Scarlet Sage, Red Velvet Sage)

planting.note: Plant container-grown or rooted cuttings in spring after last frost in partial shade to full sun; Van Houtte Sage grows vigorously in zone 9 and can reach 4–6 feet in a single season. It roots readily from tip cuttings in water or moist soil. In zone 9a, plants may be damaged by hard frost but resprout from the root crown in spring; in zone 9b-10, it overwinters reliably as a semi-evergreen perennial. Prune by half after each bloom flush to stimulate repeat blooming and maintain compact habit.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Van Houtte Sage grows fastest in full sun but tolerates and even appreciates afternoon shade in zone 9's intense summer heat, where it continues blooming through July–August when other sages shut down. Salvia van-houttei is a large, vigorous, soft-stemmed tropical sage from Brazil that produces some of the most theatrical and unusual flowers in the genus. The whorled flower spikes are covered with large, deeply-veined, velvety, dark burgundy-red to wine-colored calyxes that persist long after the scarlet corolla flowers inside them have fallen — the persistent, showy calyxes give the entire spike a dramatic deep-red to black-purple appearance and extend the ornamental interest of each bloom spike for weeks. The flowers themselves are tubular, bright scarlet, and intensely attractive to hummingbirds and large butterflies including swallowtails. The large, soft, dark-green, pebbly-textured leaves are aromatic and give the plant a bold, tropical presence even out of bloom. Van Houtte Sage is one of the most underused and undervalued of the tropical sages available for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens — combining long bloom season, hummingbird attraction, heat tolerance, and an exotic tropical appearance that rivals showier but less adaptable ornamentals.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Salvia species for Florida: tropical sages
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Salvia van-houttei for Gulf Coast hummingbird gardens
LSU AgCenter — Hummingbird Sages for Louisiana Landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Salvia van-houttei
